summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2011 annual report covers his best year ever, breaking $100,000 in total sales across Bingo Card Creator, consulting, and Appointment Reminder. BCC was in maintenance mode for most of the year but remained roughly flat at ~$23k profit. Consulting generated $55k in revenue but consumed far more time than expected due to prospecting overhead and travel. Appointment Reminder grew despite neglect, with McKenzie weighing whether to take outside investment or grow organically. He candidly discusses how disorganization and lack of focus prevented him from executing on his primary goal of building AR, and closes with a personal engagement announcement.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator generated ~$45k in sales and ~$23k profit on essentially zero active work, proving the value of durable automated systems like SEO and email marketing
Consulting revealed that raising rates from X to 5X had virtually no impact on demand, but the overhead of prospecting, travel, and deal-making consumed far more time than the actual billable work
Appointment Reminder sits at a strategic crossroads between organic growth and venture investment, with McKenzie deliberately keeping options open while his runway is infinite
Enterprise sales emerged as unexpectedly important for AR, to the point where NDAs prevent public discussion of the business numbers
Immigration issues in Japan consumed two full months, highlighting the real operational costs of being a self-employed foreigner running a business outside Silicon Valley
money_quotes: I forecast 50% growth, reasoning 'Hey, most of the systems work pretty much without my intervention, so I think the overall growth of the Internet plus a few A/B test means, oh, 50% or so.' It mostly tread water.
SEO, AdWords, email marketing, and the usual scalable marketing stuff continued to be my bread and butter even when I was too lazy to actually cut and butter bread.
My first guesstimate at my rate, back in 2010, was $X. It turns out that I could do just about as much work as I wanted regardless of whether I charged $X, $2X, or $5X.
It takes approximately X units of work to set up an engagement with a previous satisfied customer, 5X units of work to get a new prospect to the go/don't-go decision on a new engagement.
My runway is infinite, the market opportunity is only getting bigger, and the perceived value of my involvement with a startup among investors does not appear to be depreciating.
One of my customers reports that just the delta in no-shows since starting to use AR would pay for his mortgage and his daughter's college education.
I was a shoe-in for a Japanese visa back when I worked at a large megacorp, was not a shoe-in for a visa when doing my own thing.
The last 3,300 words ultimately matter much, much less than the next 3: she said yes.
key_insight: A software business built on durable, automated systems like SEO and email marketing can sustain itself with almost no active work, but growing beyond that plateau requires the focus and discipline that consulting income and life logistics constantly threaten to consume.
